metadata.title: Holistic and Automated Task Scheduling for Distributed LSM-tree-based Storage
metadata.titleTranslation: 面向分布式 LSM-tree 存储的整体式自动化任务调度
metadata.venue: 24th USENIX Conference on File and Storage Technologies (FAST '26)

sections.title: Abstract
sections.originalMarkdown: Mitigating latency fluctuations for distributed key-value (KV) stores is critical, yet it is often hindered by the tight coupling of foreground and background tasks related to data distribution and storage management. Using Cassandra, a widely deployed distributed LSM-tree-based KV store, as a case study, we observe that foreground read tasks are often interfered with by background compaction tasks, yet compaction tasks are critical for achieving high read performance. We propose HATS, a holistic and automated task scheduling framework that judiciously co-schedules read and compaction tasks, so as to mitigate latency fluctuations and achieve load balancing. HATS features coarse-grained and fine-grained replica selection for reads as well as adaptive rate control for compaction. We implement HATS atop Cassandra and demonstrate its improved latency and throughput performance over state-of-the-art distributed LSM-tree-based KV stores.
sections.translationMarkdown: 缓解分布式键值（KV）存储的延迟波动至关重要，但这往往受到与数据分布和存储管理相关的前台任务与后台任务紧密耦合的阻碍。我们以广泛部署的、基于分布式 LSM-tree 的 KV 存储 Cassandra 为案例进行研究，观察到前台读取任务经常受到后台压实任务的干扰，而压实任务对于实现较高的读取性能又至关重要。我们提出 HATS，一种整体式自动化任务调度框架，它审慎地协同调度读取任务和压实任务，以缓解延迟波动并实现负载均衡。HATS 支持面向读取的粗粒度和细粒度副本选择，以及面向压实的自适应速率控制。我们在 Cassandra 之上实现了 HATS，并证明，相较于最先进的基于分布式 LSM-tree 的 KV 存储，HATS 能够改善延迟和吞吐量性能。

sections.title: 1 Introduction
sections.originalMarkdown:
Persistent key-value (KV) stores are critical components for diverse applications, including e-commerce [22], social networks [24], and online analytical processing [14]. To accommodate the scalable and highly available I/O demands of applications, KV stores are often distributed, with multiple (storage) nodes [3, 6, 15, 43]. One deployment challenge for distributed KV stores is to provide low-latency guarantees for client I/O requests. This latency includes round-trip delays between clients and the distributed KV store (distribution layer) and the processing time within a node’s storage engine (storage layer). When a node becomes overloaded, often caused by skewed I/O access [8, 14, 53], requests can be accumulated in both distribution and storage layers, leading to increased queuing delays and, ultimately, high tail latencies [21].

Effective load balancing is important for preventing latency variability under dynamic loads in distributed KV stores, but remains challenging. Relying solely on resource usage or access patterns for load-balancing decisions does not provide low-latency guarantees. A study of YouTube’s data centers [52] shows that even if CPU loads are perfectly balanced across nodes, query latencies fluctuate over time and latency spikes still occur, as current resource usage poorly implies future request latencies. Some studies [16, 27, 39, 45, 49, 52] leverage replication to provide fault tolerance and distribute request loads across replicas, so as to mitigate hotspots and latency fluctuations. However, our analysis (§3) shows that latency fluctuations still exist despite evenly distributed I/O requests. Furthermore, nodes can be temporarily overloaded at small timescales, thereby further complicating adaptive and fine-grained load balancing.

One major root cause for latency fluctuations is the interference from background tasks in the storage layer [21, 49]. We use KV stores based on log-structured merge trees (LSM-trees) as an example, as LSM-trees have been widely adopted in production. LSM-trees perform compaction in the background to remove stale KV pairs. Such compaction tasks consume substantial CPU and disk I/O resources [33], thereby creating contention with foreground tasks and exacerbating latency fluctuations. Compaction tasks are asynchronously executed across nodes, leading to dynamic and imbalanced resource usage. However, simply rate-limiting or deferring compaction is impractical, as compaction is necessary for mitigating lookups across LSM-tree levels for high read performance. Current replication-based load-balancing designs [16, 27, 39, 45, 49, 52] mainly focus on balancing foreground tasks in the distribution layer, but pay limited attention to the interplay between foreground and background tasks in the storage layer. We argue that careful co-scheduling of foreground and background tasks is critical for effectively mitigating latency fluctuations.

We propose HATS, a holistic and automated task scheduling framework that co-schedules foreground read and background compaction tasks to provide low-latency guarantees at both large and small timescales for distributed LSM-tree-based KV stores. HATS considers a holistic view of task performance across both distribution and storage layers. It dynamically adjusts read task distributions across nodes and compaction task rates to achieve fine-grained load balancing. It performs three task scheduling operations in an iterative, closed-loop manner: (i) coarse-grained read task assignment, which periodically distributes read tasks across nodes based on measured load conditions; (ii) fine-grained read task coordination, which redirects read tasks to replica nodes based on the instantaneous load conditions; and (iii) compaction task scheduling, which launches compaction tasks based on current read loads to mitigate interference between read and compaction tasks.

We implemented HATS atop Cassandra (v5.0) [32], a widely deployed LSM-tree-based distributed KV store. HATS outperforms state-of-the-art distributed LSM-tree-based KV stores. Under YCSB read-dominant workloads [17], HATS reduces P99 latencies by 58.6% and 59.9% and achieves throughput gains of 2.41× and 2.90× compared with C3 [49] and DEPART [55], respectively. HATS also shows performance gains in various mixed workloads. Under Facebook production workloads [14], HATS reduces P99 Get latencies by 78.9% and 68.3% and increases throughput by 2.42× and 2.27× compared with C3 and DEPART, respectively. We have open-sourced our HATS prototype at https://github.com/adslabcuhk/hats.
sections.translationMarkdown:
持久化键值（KV）存储是多种应用的关键组件，包括电子商务 [22]、社交网络 [24] 和在线分析处理 [14]。为了满足应用对可扩展且高可用 I/O 的需求，KV 存储通常采用由多个（存储）节点构成的分布式架构 [3, 6, 15, 43]。分布式 KV 存储面临的一项部署挑战，是为客户端 I/O 请求提供低延迟保证。这里的延迟包括客户端与分布式 KV 存储之间的往返延迟（分布层），以及节点存储引擎内部的处理时间（存储层）。当某个节点过载时——这通常由倾斜的 I/O 访问造成 [8, 14, 53]——请求可能同时在分布层和存储层中积压，从而增加排队延迟，并最终导致较高的尾延迟 [21]。

有效的负载均衡对于防止分布式 KV 存储在动态负载下出现延迟变化十分重要，但实现起来仍具挑战。仅依赖资源使用情况或访问模式进行负载均衡决策，无法提供低延迟保证。一项针对 YouTube 数据中心的研究 [52] 表明，即使节点之间的 CPU 负载实现了完美均衡，查询延迟仍会随时间波动，延迟尖峰也依然会出现，因为当前的资源使用情况难以有效预示未来的请求延迟。一些研究 [16, 27, 39, 45, 49, 52] 利用复制提供容错能力，并将请求负载分散到各个副本，以缓解热点和延迟波动。然而，我们的分析（§3）表明，即使 I/O 请求分布均匀，延迟波动仍然存在。此外，节点可能在较小时间尺度上暂时过载，这进一步增加了自适应细粒度负载均衡的复杂性。

延迟波动的一个主要根源是存储层后台任务造成的干扰 [21, 49]。我们以基于日志结构合并树（LSM-tree）的 KV 存储为例，因为 LSM-tree 已在生产环境中得到广泛采用。LSM-tree 在后台执行压实，以移除陈旧的 KV 对。这类压实任务会消耗大量 CPU 和磁盘 I/O 资源 [33]，从而与前台任务形成资源争用并加剧延迟波动。各节点异步执行压实任务，导致资源使用情况动态变化且不均衡。然而，简单地限制压实速率或推迟压实并不可行，因为要获得较高的读取性能，压实对于减少跨 LSM-tree 层级的查找是必不可少的。当前基于复制的负载均衡设计 [16, 27, 39, 45, 49, 52] 主要关注如何均衡分布层中的前台任务，却很少关注存储层中前台任务与后台任务之间的相互作用。我们认为，审慎地协同调度前台任务与后台任务，是有效缓解延迟波动的关键。

我们提出 HATS，一种整体式自动化任务调度框架，它协同调度前台读取任务和后台压实任务，从而在大、小时间尺度上为基于分布式 LSM-tree 的 KV 存储提供低延迟保证。HATS 从整体视角考察分布层和存储层的任务性能。它动态调整节点之间的读取任务分布和压实任务速率，以实现细粒度负载均衡。它以迭代的闭环方式执行三种任务调度操作：(i) 粗粒度读取任务分配，根据测得的负载状况，周期性地将读取任务分配到各节点；(ii) 细粒度读取任务协调，根据瞬时负载状况，将读取任务重定向到副本节点；以及 (iii) 压实任务调度，根据当前读取负载启动压实任务，以缓解读取任务与压实任务之间的干扰。

我们在广泛部署的、基于分布式 LSM-tree 的 KV 存储 Cassandra（v5.0）[32] 之上实现了 HATS。HATS 的性能优于最先进的基于分布式 LSM-tree 的 KV 存储。在 YCSB 读取占主导的工作负载 [17] 下，与 C3 [49] 和 DEPART [55] 相比，HATS 分别将 P99 延迟降低了 58.6% 和 59.9%，并分别取得了 2.41× 和 2.90× 的吞吐量提升。HATS 在各种混合工作负载下同样展现出性能增益。在 Facebook 生产工作负载 [14] 下，与 C3 和 DEPART 相比，HATS 分别将 P99 Get 延迟降低了 78.9% 和 68.3%，并分别将吞吐量提升至 2.42× 和 2.27×。我们已在 https://github.com/adslabcuhk/hats 开源 HATS 原型。

sections.title: 2 Background
sections.originalMarkdown:
Our work is motivated by Cassandra [32], a widely deployed KV store used for numerous storage services [4]. Recent studies [45, 46, 49, 55] extend Cassandra to enhance storage management of production-grade, open-source distributed KV stores. We present the key components of Cassandra’s architecture relevant to our work, as shown in Figure 1.

*Figure 1: Cassandra’s architecture.*
sections.translationMarkdown:
我们的工作受到 Cassandra [32] 的启发。Cassandra 是一种广泛部署的 KV 存储，被用于众多存储服务 [4]。近期研究 [45, 46, 49, 55] 对 Cassandra 进行了扩展，以增强生产级开源分布式 KV 存储的存储管理能力。图 1 展示了与本工作相关的 Cassandra 架构关键组件。

*图 1：Cassandra 的架构。*

sections.title: 2.1 Distribution Layer
sections.originalMarkdown:
**Data distribution.** Cassandra organizes KV pairs using consistent hashing [31]. It maps the key space into a hash ring and partitions the key space into disjoint key ranges, each managed by a single node that provides storage. While a node may manage multiple small disjoint key ranges for flexible expansion, we simplify our discussion by assuming one continuous key range per node; our design still holds for multiple small disjoint key ranges per node. A KV pair is hashed to a key range based on its key and stored in the corresponding node. The mapping is decentralized, without involving a centralized controller for storage coordination.

**Replication.** To tolerate node failures, KV pairs in the same key range are replicated across multiple nodes in a clockwise direction along the hash ring. For example, Figure 1 shows an eight-node cluster with three-way replication. A key range hashed to N0 is replicated across N0, N1, and N2. We refer to the set of nodes that store the replicas of a key range as a replication group; for example, N0, N1, and N2 form a replication group for the key range hashed to N0.

**Request coordination.** When a client issues a request for a KV pair, it sends the request to a coordinator, typically a randomly selected node within the replication group. The coordinator routes the request to the relevant replica nodes. Cassandra supports user-configurable consistency levels for read and write operations, defined as the minimum number of replica nodes that must acknowledge a read or write for the operation to succeed. For each write, the coordinator replicates a KV pair to all replica nodes and completes a write operation upon receiving the specified number of acknowledgments. For each read, the coordinator sends the request to the specified number of replica nodes and completes a read operation upon receiving their responses; if some replica nodes do not respond, the coordinator sends the request to other replica nodes.

**Load balancing.** The coordinator employs dynamic snitching [5] to monitor read latencies and select replica nodes with the least current loads to serve read requests, based on the read consistency level. If the coordinator is a selected replica node, it accesses its local storage directly; otherwise, it relays the request to remote replica nodes, which return the results. For example, in Figure 1, a client contacts the coordinator N0 to read a KV pair. N0 ranks all replica nodes in the replication group by their current loads. For a read consistency level of one, N0 may select N2 to serve the request.

**Membership maintenance.** Cassandra uses the Gossip protocol [23] for cluster membership management. Nodes periodically exchange heartbeat signals with up to two peers to share liveness and load information, so as to converge to a consistent cluster-wide membership view. For fast bootstrapping of new nodes joining the cluster, Cassandra designates a few nodes as seed nodes to disseminate membership information to newcomers.
sections.translationMarkdown:
**数据分布。** Cassandra 使用一致性哈希 [31] 组织 KV 对。它将键空间映射为一个哈希环，并将键空间划分为互不相交的键范围，每个键范围由一个提供存储的节点管理。虽然为了实现灵活扩展，一个节点可以管理多个较小且互不相交的键范围，但为简化讨论，我们假设每个节点管理一个连续的键范围；对于每个节点管理多个较小且互不相交的键范围，我们的设计仍然适用。一个 KV 对根据其键被哈希到某个键范围，并存储在对应节点中。这种映射是去中心化的，不涉及用于协调存储的集中式控制器。

**复制。** 为了容忍节点故障，同一键范围内的 KV 对会沿哈希环顺时针方向复制到多个节点上。例如，图 1 展示了一个采用三副本复制的八节点集群。哈希到 N0 的键范围会复制到 N0、N1 和 N2。我们将存储某一键范围各副本的一组节点称为复制组；例如，N0、N1 和 N2 共同构成哈希到 N0 的键范围的复制组。

**请求协调。** 当客户端针对某个 KV 对发出请求时，它会将请求发送给协调节点，协调节点通常是从复制组中随机选择的节点。协调节点将请求路由到相关副本节点。Cassandra 支持用户配置读写操作的一致性级别，其定义为一次读或写操作成功所需确认该操作的最少副本节点数。对于每次写入，协调节点会将 KV 对复制到所有副本节点，并在收到指定数量的确认后完成写操作。对于每次读取，协调节点会将请求发送给指定数量的副本节点，并在收到它们的响应后完成读操作；如果某些副本节点没有响应，协调节点会将请求发送给其他副本节点。

**负载均衡。** 协调节点采用动态嗅探（dynamic snitching）[5] 监控读取延迟，并依据读取一致性级别，选择当前负载最低的副本节点来服务读取请求。如果协调节点本身是被选中的副本节点，它会直接访问本地存储；否则，它会将请求转发给远程副本节点，由远程副本节点返回结果。例如，在图 1 中，客户端联系协调节点 N0 以读取一个 KV 对。N0 根据复制组中所有副本节点的当前负载对它们进行排序。当读取一致性级别为一时，N0 可能选择 N2 来服务该请求。

**成员关系维护。** Cassandra 使用 Gossip 协议 [23] 管理集群成员关系。节点周期性地与至多两个对等节点交换心跳信号，共享存活状态和负载信息，从而收敛到一致的全局集群成员视图。为了让新加入集群的节点快速完成引导，Cassandra 指定少数节点作为种子节点，向新节点传播成员关系信息。

sections.title: 2.2 Storage Layer
sections.originalMarkdown:
Each Cassandra node manages KV pairs using an LSM-tree [41], a storage engine widely adopted in distributed storage (e.g., BigTable [15] and HBase [6]). An LSM-tree organizes KV pairs into fixed-size files called SSTables across multiple levels, and KV pairs are progressively moved from lower to higher levels. KV pairs are first written to a write-ahead log (WAL) for crash consistency and then inserted into an in-memory structure called the MemTable. When the MemTable is full, it is converted into an SSTable and flushed to the lowest level. If a level reaches a pre-specified capacity limit, the LSM-tree performs compaction, which merges SSTables from the current level to the next higher level.

To query a KV pair (Figure 1), a node first queries in-memory components, starting with the MemTable, followed by LSM-tree caches, and finally the file system cache. If the KV pair is not found in memory, the node searches on-disk SSTables from lower to higher levels and returns the result to the coordinator.
sections.translationMarkdown:
每个 Cassandra 节点都使用 LSM-tree [41] 管理 KV 对；LSM-tree 是一种在分布式存储中得到广泛采用的存储引擎（例如 BigTable [15] 和 HBase [6]）。LSM-tree 将 KV 对组织到跨多个层级的固定大小文件中，这些文件称为 SSTable，KV 对会逐步从较低层级移至较高层级。KV 对首先被写入预写日志（WAL）以保证崩溃一致性，随后插入名为 MemTable 的内存结构。当 MemTable 写满时，它会被转换为 SSTable 并刷写到最低层。如果某一层达到预先设定的容量上限，LSM-tree 就会执行压实，将当前层的 SSTable 合并到更高一层。

为了查询一个 KV 对（图 1），节点首先查询内存组件，依次从 MemTable、LSM-tree 缓存查询到文件系统缓存。如果在内存中没有找到该 KV 对，节点便从较低层到较高层搜索磁盘上的 SSTable，并将结果返回给协调节点。

sections.title: 3 Motivation
sections.originalMarkdown: We now show the sources of latency fluctuations in the distribution layer (§3.1) and the interplay between read and compaction tasks in the storage layer (§3.2). Our study focuses on the latency fluctuations of reads instead of writes, as write performance in LSM-trees is relatively stable due to in-memory buffering of KV pairs and sequential disk writes. In contrast, reads traverse multiple LSM-tree levels across different disk regions and are more prone to variability. Note that read latency fluctuations are affected by foreground and background writes (the latter are due to LSM-tree compaction), so our analysis actually takes into account the impact of writes.
sections.translationMarkdown: 下面，我们将展示分布层中延迟波动的来源（§3.1），以及存储层中读取任务与压实任务之间的相互作用（§3.2）。我们的研究聚焦于读取而非写入的延迟波动，这是因为借助 KV 对的内存缓冲和磁盘顺序写入，LSM-tree 的写入性能相对稳定。相比之下，读取需要跨越位于不同磁盘区域的多个 LSM-tree 层级，因此更容易出现性能变化。需要注意的是，读取延迟波动会受到前台写入和后台写入的影响（后者由 LSM-tree 压实引起），因此我们的分析实际上也考虑了写入的影响。

sections.title: 3.1 Latency Fluctuations
sections.originalMarkdown:
Balancing read latencies across nodes is challenging, even when resource usage and request frequencies are uniformly distributed. To validate, we generate realistic KV workloads using the YCSB benchmarking tool [17]. We pre-load 100 M 1-KiB KV pairs into a 10-node Cassandra cluster (see §6.1 for testbed details). We issue a skewed read-heavy workload based on YCSB Workload B, which has a 95:5 read-to-update ratio and follows a Zipf distribution with a Zipfian constant of 0.99, to the cluster for 30 minutes. All nodes have the same hardware configuration for uniform resource distribution. We equally shard key ranges with three-way replication for balanced request distribution.

**Observation 1: Access-frequency balance does not imply read-latency balance.** While replication mitigates skewness in access frequencies [16, 27, 39, 45, 49, 52], it does not necessarily balance read latencies across nodes (similar observations are made for multiple runs). In our read-intensive workload, access frequencies across key ranges are highly skewed, with the most accessed key range receiving 50% more requests than the least accessed. Cassandra addresses this imbalance by evenly distributing requests across replica nodes. Figure 2(a) shows the request distribution across nodes, in which the maximum difference in access frequencies across nodes is reduced to 18.9%. However, this does not resolve read-latency imbalance. Figure 2(b) shows the latency distribution across nodes, in which the highest-latency node experiences a latency increase of 4.24× compared to the lowest-latency node. This disparity suggests that equalizing access frequencies, even in a homogeneous cluster, is insufficient for balancing read performance, as other factors beyond workload distribution contribute to read-latency imbalance.

*Figure 2: Request and latency distributions.*

**Observation 2: Read latencies experience significant fluctuations at small timescales.** To further examine read-latency imbalance, we focus on the node with the highest average read latency (i.e., node 9 in Figure 2(b)) across different timescales (similar observations are made for other nodes). We measure the average read latencies of the selected node at two timescales: one-minute and one-second windows. We normalize the results by dividing the average request latency in each window by the overall average latency of the selected node over the 30-minute period. Figure 3(a) shows that latencies in one-minute windows remain relatively stable, ranging from 0.5× to 2.0× of the overall average. In contrast, Figure 3(b) shows frequent latency spikes in one-second windows, with 90.8% of the windows having latencies outside the 0.5× to 2.0× range of the overall average. These fine-grained latency fluctuations stem from the dynamic interplay of foreground workloads and system activities. Specifically, mixed read-write workloads trigger dynamic foreground I/Os and transient background tasks (e.g., compaction), which compete for CPU, DRAM, and I/O resources and cause significant latency fluctuations within nodes. The asynchronous nature of these activities across nodes leads to varying performance impacts and the observed latency imbalance across nodes (Observation 1). The results suggest that load balancing is critical at sufficiently small timescales.

*Figure 3: Normalized read latencies on the node with the highest average latency at large and small timescales.*
sections.translationMarkdown:
即使资源使用情况和请求频率均匀分布，要在节点之间均衡读取延迟也颇具挑战。为了验证这一点，我们使用 YCSB 基准测试工具 [17] 生成现实的 KV 工作负载。我们向一个 10 节点 Cassandra 集群中预加载 1 亿个大小为 1 KiB 的 KV 对（测试平台详情见 §6.1）。随后在集群上运行 30 分钟基于 YCSB Workload B 的倾斜型读取密集工作负载；该工作负载的读取与更新比例为 95:5，并遵循 Zipf 常数为 0.99 的 Zipf 分布。所有节点采用相同的硬件配置，以实现均匀的资源分布。我们对键范围进行等量分片，并采用三副本复制，以实现均衡的请求分布。

**观察 1：访问频率均衡并不意味着读取延迟均衡。** 尽管复制能够缓解访问频率的倾斜 [16, 27, 39, 45, 49, 52]，但它不一定能够均衡节点之间的读取延迟（多次运行中均得到类似观察结果）。在我们的读取密集型工作负载中，不同键范围之间的访问频率高度倾斜，其中访问量最高的键范围收到的请求比访问量最低的键范围多 50%。Cassandra 通过将请求均匀分布到各副本节点来应对这种不均衡。图 2(a) 展示了请求在各节点之间的分布，其中节点之间访问频率的最大差异被降至 18.9%。然而，这并没有解决读取延迟不均衡问题。图 2(b) 展示了各节点的延迟分布，其中延迟最高节点的延迟是延迟最低节点的 4.24×。这种差异说明，即使在同构集群中，仅仅使访问频率相等也不足以均衡读取性能，因为除工作负载分布外，还有其他因素会造成读取延迟不均衡。

*图 2：请求和延迟分布。*

**观察 2：读取延迟在小时间尺度上会发生显著波动。** 为进一步研究读取延迟不均衡，我们在不同时间尺度上考察平均读取延迟最高的节点（即图 2(b) 中的节点 9；其他节点也呈现类似现象）。我们在两种时间尺度上测量所选节点的平均读取延迟：一分钟窗口和一秒钟窗口。我们将每个窗口中的平均请求延迟除以所选节点在整个 30 分钟期间的总体平均延迟，从而对结果进行归一化。图 3(a) 表明，一分钟窗口中的延迟相对稳定，处于总体平均值的 0.5× 至 2.0× 范围内。相比之下，图 3(b) 显示一秒钟窗口中频繁出现延迟尖峰，其中 90.8% 的窗口延迟落在总体平均值的 0.5× 至 2.0× 范围之外。这些细粒度延迟波动源自前台工作负载与系统活动之间的动态相互作用。具体而言，混合读写工作负载会触发动态前台 I/O 和短暂的后台任务（例如压实），它们争用 CPU、DRAM 和 I/O 资源，并在节点内部造成显著的延迟波动。这些活动在各节点之间具有异步性，从而造成不同的性能影响，并产生观察到的节点间延迟不均衡（观察 1）。这些结果表明，在足够小的时间尺度上进行负载均衡至关重要。

*图 3：在大、小时间尺度下，平均延迟最高节点上的归一化读取延迟。*

sections.title: 3.2 Coupling of Read and Compaction Tasks
sections.titleTranslation: 3.2 读取任务与压实任务的耦合
sections.originalMarkdown:
While LSM-tree compaction is resource-intensive and known to compete for resources with read tasks [33, 34, 50], it is essential for maintaining high long-term read performance. To understand the interplay of read and compaction tasks under workload skewness and asynchronous compaction, we analyze aggregate interference across all nodes in the same 10-node Cassandra cluster pre-loaded with 100 M 1-KiB KV pairs (§3.1). We warm up the cluster by compacting the LSM-trees of all nodes, followed by 50 M updates and 30 minutes of reads (both updates and reads follow a Zipf distribution with a Zipfian constant of 0.99). We disable compaction for the first 10 minutes, enable it for the next 10 minutes, and disable it again for the final 10 minutes. Figure 4 shows the aggregate read throughput (in KOPS) and compaction throughput (in MiB/s) across the cluster every minute; their inverse relationship shows task interference.

**Observation 3: Compaction tasks create resource contention with read tasks.** When compaction is enabled at the 10th minute, the read throughput drops from 26.3 KOPS to 7.3 KOPS within two minutes due to significant resource contention between read and compaction tasks. Between the 17th and 18th minutes, the compaction throughput drops due to Cassandra’s internal rate-limiting, which recovers read throughput from 7.1 KOPS to 11 KOPS.

*Figure 4: Read and compaction throughput over time.*

**Observation 4: Compaction tasks are crucial for improving read performance.** By comparing the read throughput in the first and last 10 minutes (when compaction is disabled), we observe an increase in the average read throughput from 29.8 KOPS to 40.7 KOPS. This improvement is due to compaction tasks between the 10th and 20th minutes, which merge SSTables across levels and reduce the number of SSTables searched during reads. In both periods, the read throughput starts low and increases sharply due to cache warm-up, but drops after a few minutes (3 minutes in the first period and 5 minutes in the last period), as reading more SSTables from disk triggers page-cache swapping. This issue is more pronounced in the first 10 minutes, with 35.9% throughput drop, compared to 21% drop in the last 10 minutes.
sections.translationMarkdown:
尽管 LSM-tree 压实需要消耗大量资源，而且已知会与读取任务争用资源 [33, 34, 50]，但它对于长期维持较高的读取性能必不可少。为了理解工作负载倾斜和异步压实条件下读取任务与压实任务之间的相互作用，我们在同一个预加载了 1 亿个 1 KiB KV 对的 10 节点 Cassandra 集群（§3.1）中，分析所有节点上的总体干扰。我们首先通过压实所有节点上的 LSM-tree 来预热集群，随后执行 5000 万次更新和 30 分钟的读取（更新和读取均遵循 Zipf 常数为 0.99 的 Zipf 分布）。在最初 10 分钟禁用压实，接下来的 10 分钟启用压实，并在最后 10 分钟再次禁用压实。图 4 展示了集群每分钟的总体读取吞吐量（单位为 KOPS）和压实吞吐量（单位为 MiB/s）；二者之间的反向关系表明了任务干扰。

**观察 3：压实任务会与读取任务产生资源争用。** 在第 10 分钟启用压实后，由于读取任务和压实任务之间发生显著的资源争用，读取吞吐量在两分钟内从 26.3 KOPS 降至 7.3 KOPS。在第 17 分钟到第 18 分钟之间，受 Cassandra 内部速率限制机制影响，压实吞吐量下降，使读取吞吐量从 7.1 KOPS 恢复至 11 KOPS。

*图 4：读取吞吐量和压实吞吐量随时间的变化。*

**观察 4：压实任务对于改善读取性能至关重要。** 通过比较最初和最后 10 分钟（压实均被禁用）的读取吞吐量，我们观察到平均读取吞吐量从 29.8 KOPS 提升至 40.7 KOPS。这一提升源自第 10 分钟至第 20 分钟执行的压实任务，它们合并跨层的 SSTable，并减少读取期间需要搜索的 SSTable 数量。在这两个时间段中，读取吞吐量起初都较低，随后因缓存预热而急剧上升，但几分钟后又出现下降（第一个时间段为 3 分钟后，最后一个时间段为 5 分钟后），这是因为从磁盘读取更多 SSTable 触发了页缓存换入换出。该问题在最初 10 分钟更为明显，吞吐量下降了 35.9%；相比之下，最后 10 分钟的下降幅度为 21%。

sections.title: 4 HATS Design
sections.originalMarkdown: Existing resource-driven load balancers (e.g., [38]) can achieve load balancing at the node level, but current resource metrics often poorly predict future request latencies at the request level [52]. Also, they do not address the crucial interplay between foreground tasks in the distribution layer and background tasks in the storage layer. HATS is a holistic load balancer that mitigates latency fluctuations at both node and request levels through careful co-scheduling of foreground and background tasks.
sections.translationMarkdown: 现有的资源驱动型负载均衡器（例如 [38]）可以在节点级实现负载均衡，但当前的资源指标往往难以在请求级准确预测未来的请求延迟 [52]。此外，它们没有处理分布层前台任务与存储层后台任务之间至关重要的相互作用。HATS 是一种整体式负载均衡器，通过谨慎地协同调度前台和后台任务，在节点级和请求级缓解延迟波动。

sections.title: 4.1 Design Overview
sections.originalMarkdown:
HATS takes a closed-loop approach and iteratively performs three task scheduling operations, as shown in Figure 5. At a high level, coarse-grained read task assignment periodically balances read loads across nodes at regular intervals (called epochs), using a global view of current read loads to address predictable imbalances (e.g., workload skewness or straggler nodes) over large timescales. Within each epoch, fine-grained read task coordination dynamically adjusts instantaneous read loads for each replication group over small timescales to provide low-latency guarantees for individual requests; and compaction task scheduling prioritizes compaction for key ranges with high read loads to maintain high read performance while minimizing interference. This closed-loop design combines read distribution, compaction rates, and dynamic replica selection to mitigate latency fluctuations.

**Figure 5:** Overview of HATS’s workflow. HATS employs replica decoupling [55] to separate replicas into distinct LSM-trees to facilitate compaction task scheduling (§4.4).

HATS integrates task scheduling operations into Cassandra’s I/O flows. It ensures reliability and scalability in task scheduling, without compromising the correctness of Cassandra’s operations. It focuses on co-scheduling read and compaction tasks, while processing write tasks as in Cassandra; that is, clients distribute writes across replica nodes via consistent hashing, and nodes buffer KV pairs for sequential writes to the WAL and SSTables. We emphasize that HATS never disregards writes, as it mitigates read latency fluctuations affected by foreground and background writes. In write-intensive workloads, more compaction tasks are triggered and introduce high read latency fluctuations. HATS shows performance gains in this scenario by co-scheduling read and compaction tasks (see evaluation results of write-intensive workloads (YCSB-A/F) in §6).

**Applicability.** HATS builds on Cassandra and addresses two fundamental issues: latency fluctuations (§3.1) and task coupling (§3.2). Its design is applicable to other distributed LSM-tree-based KV stores that employ replication for fault tolerance. HATS relies on two key components in its deployment. First, it leverages replica decoupling, a technique validated in LSM-tree-based systems [34, 50, 55]. In Cassandra, replica decoupling can be implemented with around 150 lines of code, including modifications to both the read and write paths to manage multiple LSM-trees per node. Modern LSM-tree-based systems (e.g., RocksDB and Cassandra) natively support multiple tables per node, with each table mapped to a distinct LSM-tree, thereby facilitating the mapping of decoupled replicas to separate LSM-trees. Also, replica decoupling has limited overhead (only 0.4% of write time [55]), while offering a rich design space for mitigating latency fluctuations in distributed settings. Second, HATS employs replica selection [49], which can build on either eventual consistency (e.g., Cassandra, Dynamo [22], and ScyllaDB [47]) or follower reads [42] with strong consistency (e.g., TiKV [43]); both techniques are widely adopted in production distributed storage systems.

While HATS targets LSM-tree-based storage, other storage systems (e.g., B+-tree-based) also experience interference between foreground read/writes and background index maintenance. Thus, HATS is motivated by the very existence of the interference issue. Also, even though high-performance distributed KV stores have been proposed (e.g., [29, 56]), they do not consider the issues of latency fluctuations and task coupling as studied in this paper. HATS’s design complements them for further performance gains.

**Setup.** Consider a cluster of $M$ nodes, $N_0, N_1, \ldots, N_{M-1}$, arranged consecutively along the clockwise direction on a hash ring. Let $R$ be the replication factor, where the $R$ replicas of $N_i$ ($0 \le i < M$) are stored in $N_i, N_{i+1}, \ldots, N_{i+R-1}$ (we omit “modulo $M$” in the subscripts for brevity). Thus, each node stores its own replica and $R-1$ replicas originating from the previous $R-1$ nodes in the anti-clockwise direction on the hash ring. Let $K_{i,j}$ be the $j$-th replica of the key range that is hashed to $N_i$ and stored in $N_{i+j}$, where $0 \le j < R$. For example, $K_{i,0}$ is the replica of the key range hashed to $N_i$, and $K_{i-1,1}$ is the replica of the key range hashed to $N_{i-1}$ and stored in $N_i$. Let $L$ be the epoch length at which HATS periodically performs coarse-grained load balancing; we discuss the configuration of $L$ in §5.
sections.translationMarkdown:
HATS 采用闭环方法，并如图 5 所示迭代执行三种任务调度操作。总体而言，粗粒度读取任务分配以固定间隔（称为 epoch）周期性地均衡节点间的读取负载，利用当前读取负载的全局视图来处理大时间尺度上可预测的不均衡（例如工作负载倾斜或拖尾节点）。在每个 epoch 内，细粒度读取任务协调在小时间尺度上动态调整每个复制组的瞬时读取负载，从而为单个请求提供低延迟保证；压实任务调度则优先压实读取负载较高的键范围，以在最大限度减少干扰的同时维持较高的读取性能。这种闭环设计结合读取分布、压实速率和动态副本选择来缓解延迟波动。

**图 5：** HATS 工作流程概述。HATS 采用副本解耦 [55]，将副本分离到不同的 LSM-tree 中，以便进行压实任务调度（§4.4）。

HATS 将任务调度操作集成到 Cassandra 的 I/O 流程中。它在不损害 Cassandra 操作正确性的前提下，确保任务调度的可靠性和可扩展性。它侧重于协同调度读取与压实任务，同时以与 Cassandra 相同的方式处理写入任务；也就是说，客户端通过一致性哈希将写入分发到副本节点，而节点缓冲 KV 对，以便顺序写入 WAL 和 SSTable。我们强调，HATS 从不忽视写入，因为它所缓解的读取延迟波动会受到前台和后台写入的影响。在写密集型工作负载中，会触发更多压实任务，并引入剧烈的读取延迟波动。HATS 通过协同调度读取和压实任务，在这种场景下取得性能增益（参见 §6 中写密集型工作负载（YCSB-A/F）的评估结果）。

**适用性。** HATS 构建于 Cassandra 之上，并解决两个根本问题：延迟波动（§3.1）和任务耦合（§3.2）。其设计适用于其他通过复制实现容错的分布式 LSM-tree 型 KV 存储。HATS 的部署依赖两个关键组件。首先，它利用副本解耦，这项技术已在 LSM-tree 型系统中得到验证 [34, 50, 55]。在 Cassandra 中，副本解耦可以用大约 150 行代码实现，其中包括对读写路径的修改，以管理每个节点上的多个 LSM-tree。现代 LSM-tree 型系统（例如 RocksDB 和 Cassandra）原生支持每个节点包含多个表，每个表映射到一个独立的 LSM-tree，从而便于将解耦后的副本映射到不同的 LSM-tree。此外，副本解耦的开销有限（仅占写入时间的 0.4% [55]），同时为缓解分布式环境中的延迟波动提供了丰富的设计空间。其次，HATS 采用副本选择 [49]，它既可以构建在最终一致性之上（例如 Cassandra、Dynamo [22] 和 ScyllaDB [47]），也可以采用具有强一致性的跟随者读取 [42]（例如 TiKV [43]）；这两种技术都已广泛应用于生产级分布式存储系统。

虽然 HATS 面向 LSM-tree 型存储，但其他存储系统（例如基于 B+-tree 的系统）同样会遭遇前台读写与后台索引维护之间的干扰。因此，干扰问题本身的存在正是 HATS 的设计动机。此外，尽管已经提出了高性能分布式 KV 存储（例如 [29, 56]），但它们没有考虑本文研究的延迟波动和任务耦合问题。HATS 的设计可与这些系统互补，以进一步获得性能提升。

**系统设定。** 考虑一个由 $M$ 个节点 $N_0, N_1, \ldots, N_{M-1}$ 构成的集群，这些节点沿哈希环顺时针方向依次排列。令 $R$ 为复制因子，其中 $N_i$（$0 \le i < M$）的 $R$ 个副本存储在 $N_i, N_{i+1}, \ldots, N_{i+R-1}$ 上（为简洁起见，省略下标中的“模 $M$”）。因此，每个节点都存储自己的副本，以及源自哈希环逆时针方向前 $R-1$ 个节点的 $R-1$ 个副本。令 $K_{i,j}$ 表示哈希到 $N_i$ 的键范围的第 $j$ 个副本，该副本存储在 $N_{i+j}$ 上，其中 $0 \le j < R$。例如，$K_{i,0}$ 是哈希到 $N_i$ 的键范围的副本，而 $K_{i-1,1}$ 是哈希到 $N_{i-1}$ 并存储在 $N_i$ 上的键范围副本。令 $L$ 为 HATS 周期性执行粗粒度负载均衡的 epoch 长度；我们将在 §5 中讨论 $L$ 的配置。

sections.title: 4.2 Coarse-grained Read Task Assignment
sections.originalMarkdown: HATS performs coarse-grained load balancing via periodic read task assignment in three steps: (i) constructing a global view of current read loads of all nodes, called the current state, (ii) computing balanced read loads for all nodes, called the expected state, and (iii) propagating the expected state via the Gossip protocol to all nodes.
sections.translationMarkdown: HATS 通过周期性读取任务分配，分三个步骤执行粗粒度负载均衡：(i) 构建所有节点当前读取负载的全局视图，称为当前状态；(ii) 计算所有节点均衡后的读取负载，称为预期状态；(iii) 通过 Gossip 协议将预期状态传播到所有节点。

sections.title: 4.2.1 Monitoring and Sharing of States
sections.originalMarkdown:
**Extended Gossip protocol.** HATS augments Cassandra’s Gossip protocol (§2.1) to achieve reliable task scheduling without sacrificing scalability, by asynchronously sharing both current and expected states. It designates a seed node, called the scheduler node, which constructs the current state by collecting read loads from all other nodes via the Gossip protocol, adjusts the current state into the expected state, and distributes the expected state via the Gossip protocol.

HATS ensures reliable scheduling by preventing the scheduler node from becoming either a performance bottleneck or a single-point failure. It uses the Raft consensus protocol [40] to elect a leader among seed nodes to serve as the scheduler node and re-elect a new leader if the current scheduler node fails. Raft initiates a new term (uniquely identified by an increasing term number) in each leader election. In practice, a Cassandra data center has only two or three seed nodes [2], so the leader maintenance overhead is minimal. If all current seed nodes fail, Cassandra will designate other nodes as seed nodes. Note that HATS modifies only Cassandra’s internal task scheduling policies and maintains the correctness of core operations. When a storage node fails, Cassandra’s built-in failure detection and recovery mechanisms (e.g., hinted handoff and read repair) are still in place to maintain data availability and consistency, regardless of whether HATS is enabled.

**Asynchronous state sharing.** Each node $N_i$ ($0 \le i < M$) monitors its read loads, including the average read latency (i.e., the average time of processing read requests in the storage layer) and number of read requests served for each of $R$ key ranges (i.e., $K_{i,0}, K_{i-1,1}, \ldots, K_{i-R+1,R-1}$) in $N_i$, over an epoch of length $L$. Within each epoch, each node embeds the measured average read latency (4 bytes) and numbers of read requests for $R$ key ranges ($4 \times R$ bytes) in Gossip messages, together with a locally increasing version number (4 bytes). Each node increments its local version number every second (the default Gossip interval in Cassandra) and disseminates read loads to other nodes via Gossip messages. It keeps the latest read loads with the largest version number included in the Gossip messages from other nodes.

At the end of each epoch, the scheduler node forms the current state by taking a snapshot of the currently received read loads from all nodes. It adjusts the read distribution based on the current state to form the expected state ($4 \times M \times R$ bytes) (§4.2.2) and distributes the expected state, together with the Raft term number (4 bytes) and a globally increasing epoch number (4 bytes), via Gossip messages. The epoch number is initialized as zero by the scheduler node at the beginning of a Raft term. Each node keeps the latest expected state with the largest Raft term number and epoch number. It also notifies all clients to perform request routing based on the latest expected state.

**Analysis of network overhead.** HATS incurs limited network overhead in adding information for state monitoring and sharing to Gossip messages. Based on the codebase of Cassandra (v5.0) [3], a Gossip message from each node contains $M$ entries, each describing the information about a node (e.g., heartbeat details and host ID) and has a size of 211 bytes. For a cluster of $M$ nodes, the total size of a Gossip message from each node is $211 \times M$ bytes. HATS adds $(8 + 4 \times R) \times M$ bytes for the monitored read loads of each node and $(8 + 4 \times M \times R)$ bytes for the expected state to a Gossip message. Thus, the additional overhead of a Gossip message from each node is $\frac{8 \times (M \times R + M + 1)}{211 \times M}$. For example, when $R = 3$ and $M = 100$, the overhead is 15.2%.
sections.translationMarkdown:
**扩展的 Gossip 协议。** HATS 扩展 Cassandra 的 Gossip 协议（§2.1），通过异步共享当前状态和预期状态，在不牺牲可扩展性的情况下实现可靠的任务调度。它指定一个称为调度器节点的种子节点；该节点通过 Gossip 协议收集其他所有节点的读取负载来构建当前状态，将当前状态调整为预期状态，并通过 Gossip 协议分发预期状态。

HATS 通过避免调度器节点成为性能瓶颈或单点故障来保证调度可靠性。它使用 Raft 共识协议 [40] 在种子节点中选举领导者作为调度器节点，并在当前调度器节点发生故障时重新选举新的领导者。Raft 在每次领导者选举时启动一个新任期（由递增的任期编号唯一标识）。实践中，一个 Cassandra 数据中心仅有两到三个种子节点 [2]，因此领导者维护开销很小。如果当前所有种子节点均发生故障，Cassandra 会指定其他节点作为种子节点。需要注意的是，HATS 只修改 Cassandra 的内部任务调度策略，并保持核心操作的正确性。当存储节点发生故障时，无论是否启用 HATS，Cassandra 内置的故障检测和恢复机制（例如提示移交和读取修复）仍会继续工作，以维持数据可用性和一致性。

**异步状态共享。** 每个节点 $N_i$（$0 \le i < M$）在长度为 $L$ 的一个 epoch 内监控其读取负载，其中包括平均读取延迟（即在存储层处理读取请求的平均时间），以及 $N_i$ 中 $R$ 个键范围（即 $K_{i,0}, K_{i-1,1}, \ldots, K_{i-R+1,R-1}$）各自已服务的读取请求数。在每个 epoch 内，每个节点将测得的平均读取延迟（4 字节）和 $R$ 个键范围的读取请求数（$4 \times R$ 字节）嵌入 Gossip 消息，并附带一个本地递增的版本号（4 字节）。每个节点每秒递增其本地版本号（Cassandra 中的默认 Gossip 间隔），并通过 Gossip 消息向其他节点传播读取负载。对于来自其他节点的 Gossip 消息，它保留其中版本号最大的最新读取负载。

在每个 epoch 结束时，调度器节点对当前已接收的所有节点读取负载取快照，从而形成当前状态。它根据当前状态调整读取分布，形成预期状态（$4 \times M \times R$ 字节）（§4.2.2），并通过 Gossip 消息分发预期状态以及 Raft 任期编号（4 字节）和全局递增的 epoch 编号（4 字节）。在一个 Raft 任期开始时，调度器节点将 epoch 编号初始化为零。每个节点保留具有最大 Raft 任期编号和 epoch 编号的最新预期状态。它还通知所有客户端基于最新预期状态执行请求路由。

**网络开销分析。** HATS 在 Gossip 消息中添加状态监控与共享信息所产生的网络开销有限。根据 Cassandra（v5.0）代码库 [3]，每个节点发出的 Gossip 消息包含 $M$ 个条目，每个条目描述一个节点的信息（例如心跳详情和主机 ID），大小为 211 字节。对于包含 $M$ 个节点的集群，每个节点发出的 Gossip 消息总大小为 $211 \times M$ 字节。HATS 在一条 Gossip 消息中为每个节点受监控的读取负载增加 $(8 + 4 \times R) \times M$ 字节，并为预期状态增加 $(8 + 4 \times M \times R)$ 字节。因此，每个节点发出的 Gossip 消息的额外开销为 $\frac{8 \times (M \times R + M + 1)}{211 \times M}$。例如，当 $R = 3$ 且 $M = 100$ 时，开销为 15.2%。

sections.title: 4.2.2 Adjustment of Current State
sections.originalMarkdown:
Given the current state, the scheduler node adjusts the read distribution for the $R$ replica nodes in a replication group, moves read requests from high-load nodes to low-load nodes. Let $C = [C_{i,j}]$ be the current state represented as an $M \times R$ matrix, where $C_{i,j}$ ($0 \le i < M$ and $0 \le j < R$) represents the read request count of $K_{i,j}$ (i.e., the $j$-th replica of the key range hashed to $N_i$), and let $T_i$ be the average read latency of $N_i$. Both $C_{i,j}$ and $T_i$ are obtained from the Gossip protocol.

The scheduler node classifies each node $N_i$ as high-load or low-load. For an epoch of length $L$, $L/T_i$ represents the expected number of read requests served by $N_i$, while $\sum_{j=0}^{R-1} C_{i-j,j}$ represents the actual total number of read requests served by $N_i$ in an epoch (recall that $N_i$ holds the replicas $K_{i,0}, K_{i-1,1}, \ldots, K_{i-R+1,R-1}$). If $\sum_{j=0}^{R-1} C_{i-j,j} > L/T_i$, it implies that $N_i$ receives more requests than the expected number of read requests and may see degraded performance due to increased I/O queueing delays; we call $N_i$ high-load. Otherwise, if $\sum_{j=0}^{R-1} C_{i-j,j} \le L/T_i$, it implies that $N_i$ has spare capacity to serve more read requests; we call $N_i$ low-load.

**Algorithm 1: Adjusting current state into expected state.**

**Input:** Current state $C$, and average read latency $T_i$ for all $N_i$  
**Output:** Expected state $E$

```text
1: Initialize E = C
2: Initialize Δ_i = L/T_i - Σ_{j=0}^{R-1} C_{i-j,j}
3: for N_i ∈ {N_0, …, N_{M-1}} do
4:     Set H = {N_{i+h} | Δ_{i+h} < 0, h ∈ [0,R)}
5:     Set L = {N_{i+ℓ} | Δ_{i+ℓ} ≥ 0, ℓ ∈ [0,R)}.
6:     for N_{i+h} in H do
7:         for N_{i+ℓ} in L do
8:             δ = min(|Δ_{i+h}|, |Δ_{i+ℓ}|, E_{i,h})
9:             E_{i,h} = E_{i,h} - δ
10:            E_{i,ℓ} = E_{i,ℓ} + δ
11:            Δ_{i+h} = Δ_{i+h} + δ
12:            Δ_{i+ℓ} = Δ_{i+ℓ} - δ
```

**Algorithm details.** Algorithm 1 shows the pseudo-code of how the scheduler node adjusts the current state into the expected state denoted by $E = [E_{i,j}]$, an $M \times R$ matrix in which $E_{i,j}$ ($0 \le i < M$ and $0 \le j < R$) represents the expected read request count of $K_{i,j}$. The scheduler node initializes both $E = C$ (i.e., $E_{i,j} = C_{i,j}$ for all $i$ and $j$) and $\Delta_i = L/T_i - \sum_{j=0}^{R-1} C_{i-j,j}$ (Lines 1-2). It identifies the sets of high-load and low-load replica nodes of the key range hashed to $N_i$, denoted by $\mathcal{H}$ and $\mathcal{L}$, respectively (Lines 4-5).

The scheduler node greedily transfers read requests from high-load nodes and low-load nodes. For each pair of high-load node $N_{i+h} \in \mathcal{H}$ and low-load node $N_{i+\ell} \in \mathcal{L}$, the scheduler node determines the number of requests to transfer, $\delta$, by the minimum of $|\Delta_{i+h}|$, $|\Delta_{i+\ell}|$, and $E_{i,h}$ (Line 8). It decrements $E_{i,h}$ by $\delta$ and increments $E_{i,\ell}$ by $\delta$ to move read load from $N_{i+h}$ to $N_{i+\ell}$ (Lines 9-10). It also increments $\Delta_{i+h}$ by $\delta$ and decrements $\Delta_{i+\ell}$ by $\delta$ to reflect the new load differences compared to $L/T_{i+h}$ and $L/T_{i+\ell}$, respectively (Lines 11-12). At the end of the algorithm, $E$ represents the balanced read request distribution and will be propagated to the cluster.

**Analysis of computational overhead.** The scheduler node incurs limited computational overhead compared to regular nodes. It executes Algorithm 1 once per epoch by iterating through all $M$ nodes and their $R$ replicas. For each node, the worst case is to evenly split $R$ replicas into $R/2$ high-load and $R/2$ low-load nodes, leading to $R^2/4$ pairs for adjustments. The time complexity is $O(MR^2/4)$, which is negligible due to infrequent execution (once per epoch) and small $R$ (e.g., $R = 3$).
sections.translationMarkdown:
给定当前状态后，调度器节点调整一个复制组中 $R$ 个副本节点的读取分布，将读取请求从高负载节点转移到低负载节点。令 $C = [C_{i,j}]$ 表示以 $M \times R$ 矩阵表示的当前状态，其中 $C_{i,j}$（$0 \le i < M$ 且 $0 \le j < R$）表示 $K_{i,j}$（即哈希到 $N_i$ 的键范围的第 $j$ 个副本）的读取请求数，并令 $T_i$ 表示 $N_i$ 的平均读取延迟。$C_{i,j}$ 和 $T_i$ 均通过 Gossip 协议获得。

调度器节点将每个节点 $N_i$ 分类为高负载或低负载。对于长度为 $L$ 的一个 epoch，$L/T_i$ 表示预期由 $N_i$ 服务的读取请求数，而 $\sum_{j=0}^{R-1} C_{i-j,j}$ 表示一个 epoch 内实际由 $N_i$ 服务的读取请求总数（回想一下，$N_i$ 持有副本 $K_{i,0}, K_{i-1,1}, \ldots, K_{i-R+1,R-1}$）。如果 $\sum_{j=0}^{R-1} C_{i-j,j} > L/T_i$，则表明 $N_i$ 接收的请求多于预期读取请求数，并可能因 I/O 排队延迟增加而出现性能下降；我们称 $N_i$ 为高负载。否则，如果 $\sum_{j=0}^{R-1} C_{i-j,j} \le L/T_i$，则表明 $N_i$ 有剩余容量可服务更多读取请求；我们称 $N_i$ 为低负载。

**算法 1：将当前状态调整为预期状态。**

**输入：** 当前状态 $C$，以及所有 $N_i$ 的平均读取延迟 $T_i$  
**输出：** 预期状态 $E$

```text
1: 初始化 E = C
2: 初始化 Δ_i = L/T_i - Σ_{j=0}^{R-1} C_{i-j,j}
3: 对 N_i ∈ {N_0, …, N_{M-1}} 执行
4:     设置 H = {N_{i+h} | Δ_{i+h} < 0, h ∈ [0,R)}
5:     设置 L = {N_{i+ℓ} | Δ_{i+ℓ} ≥ 0, ℓ ∈ [0,R)}。
6:     对 H 中的 N_{i+h} 执行
7:         对 L 中的 N_{i+ℓ} 执行
8:             δ = min(|Δ_{i+h}|, |Δ_{i+ℓ}|, E_{i,h})
9:             E_{i,h} = E_{i,h} - δ
10:            E_{i,ℓ} = E_{i,ℓ} + δ
11:            Δ_{i+h} = Δ_{i+h} + δ
12:            Δ_{i+ℓ} = Δ_{i+ℓ} - δ
```

**算法细节。** 算法 1 给出了调度器节点如何将当前状态调整为预期状态的伪代码。预期状态记为 $E = [E_{i,j}]$，它是一个 $M \times R$ 矩阵，其中 $E_{i,j}$（$0 \le i < M$ 且 $0 \le j < R$）表示 $K_{i,j}$ 的预期读取请求数。调度器节点同时初始化 $E = C$（即对所有 $i$ 和 $j$，均有 $E_{i,j} = C_{i,j}$）以及 $\Delta_i = L/T_i - \sum_{j=0}^{R-1} C_{i-j,j}$（第 1-2 行）。它识别哈希到 $N_i$ 的键范围所对应的高负载和低负载副本节点集合，分别记为 $\mathcal{H}$ 和 $\mathcal{L}$（第 4-5 行）。

调度器节点采用贪心方式在高负载节点和低负载节点之间转移读取请求。对于每一对高负载节点 $N_{i+h} \in \mathcal{H}$ 和低负载节点 $N_{i+\ell} \in \mathcal{L}$，调度器节点取 $|\Delta_{i+h}|$、$|\Delta_{i+\ell}|$ 和 $E_{i,h}$ 三者中的最小值，确定要转移的请求数 $\delta$（第 8 行）。它将 $E_{i,h}$ 减少 $\delta$，并将 $E_{i,\ell}$ 增加 $\delta$，从而把读取负载从 $N_{i+h}$ 转移到 $N_{i+\ell}$（第 9-10 行）。它还将 $\Delta_{i+h}$ 增加 $\delta$，并将 $\Delta_{i+\ell}$ 减少 $\delta$，以反映相对于 $L/T_{i+h}$ 和 $L/T_{i+\ell}$ 的新负载差值（第 11-12 行）。算法结束时，$E$ 表示均衡后的读取请求分布，并将被传播到整个集群。

**计算开销分析。** 与普通节点相比，调度器节点产生的计算开销有限。它每个 epoch 执行一次算法 1，遍历全部 $M$ 个节点及其 $R$ 个副本。对于每个节点，最坏情况是将 $R$ 个副本平均划分为 $R/2$ 个高负载节点和 $R/2$ 个低负载节点，从而产生 $R^2/4$ 对调整组合。时间复杂度为 $O(MR^2/4)$；由于执行频率低（每个 epoch 一次）且 $R$ 较小（例如 $R = 3$），该开销可以忽略。

sections.title: 4.2.3 Read Task Assignment
sections.originalMarkdown: When a client receives a new expected state $E$, it updates the distribution of read requests to replica nodes. For a read request to a key range hashed to $N_i$, the client randomly selects a replica node $N_{i+j}$ ($0 \le j < R$) as the coordinator with a probability $E_{i,j}/\sum_{j=0}^{R-1} E_{i,j}$. This achieves the expected load balancing across the cluster.
sections.translationMarkdown: 当客户端收到新的预期状态 $E$ 时，它会更新发往副本节点的读取请求分布。对于访问哈希到 $N_i$ 的键范围的读取请求，客户端以概率 $E_{i,j}/\sum_{j=0}^{R-1} E_{i,j}$ 随机选择一个副本节点 $N_{i+j}$（$0 \le j < R$）作为协调器。由此在整个集群中实现预期的负载均衡。

sections.title: 4.3 Fine-grained Read Task Coordination
sections.originalMarkdown:
Since read latencies can fluctuate at small timescales lead to node latency imbalances (Observation 2 in §3.1), HATS performs fine-grained load balancing. However, selecting the fastest replica may lead to load oscillations and increased tail latencies [49]. Thus, HATS employs load-aware replica selection, which fine-tunes the read request distribution based on the instantaneous load condition of each node.

When the coordinator (selected as described in §4.2.3) routes a read request for the key range hashed to $N_i$ to the $j$-th replica $K_{i,j}$ in $N_{i+j}$ ($0 \le j < R$), it monitors the instantaneous read latency $t_{i,j}$, which includes the network transmission time between the coordinator and $N_{i+j}$ and the processing time in the storage layer of $N_{i+j}$, thereby addressing the combined effects of network and storage performance. To initialize $t_{i,j}$, the coordinator selects a replica node from the replication group of a key range in a round-robin manner for the first $R$ requests in each epoch. It then updates $t_{i,j}$ based on the dynamic snitching module (§2.1) using an exponential weighted moving average, with a weight of 0.5 in our current implementation.

HATS assigns each replica node a unified score, which quantifies the capacity of a replica node to handle extra read requests. For each node $N_i$, let $Q_i = \sum_{j=0}^{R-1} E_{i-j,j}$ be the expected total number of read requests served by $N_i$ ($0 \le i < M$) based on the latest expected state. Then, the coordinator calculates the unified score for $K_{i,j}$ as $L/t_{i,j} - Q_{i+j}$, with the following physical meaning: $L/t_{i,j}$ represents the instantaneous number of read requests that can be served by $N_{i+j}$ (which stores $K_{i,j}$) over an epoch of length $L$, while $Q_{i+j}$ represents the expected number of read requests to be served. Thus, the unified score represents the number of extra read requests that $N_{i+j}$ can process under the current load condition. A higher unified score implies that a replica node has more capacity to process extra read requests. To achieve fine-grained read task coordination, the coordinator re-routes a read request to the replica node with the highest unified score among all replica nodes. Note that this process incurs negligible overhead to vanilla Cassandra, as HATS obtains instantaneous latencies from Cassandra’s dynamic snitching module and its operations are similar to those already performed by Cassandra for load balancing (§2.1).

At large timescales, the coordinator often has the highest unified score as local access incurs no network transmission, so the read loads converge to the expected state (§4.2). At small timescales, replica nodes with well-compacted LSM-trees often have small read latencies in the storage layer and become more likely to be selected by the coordinator. Thus, HATS aligns replica selection with compaction task distribution (§4.4) to mitigate latency fluctuations.
sections.translationMarkdown:
由于读取延迟可能在小时间尺度上波动并导致节点间的延迟不均衡（§3.1 中的观察 2），HATS 执行细粒度负载均衡。然而，选择最快的副本可能导致负载振荡并增大尾延迟 [49]。因此，HATS 采用负载感知的副本选择，根据各节点的瞬时负载状况对读取请求分布进行微调。

当协调器（按 §4.2.3 所述方式选择）将一个访问哈希到 $N_i$ 的键范围的读取请求，路由到 $N_{i+j}$ 中的第 $j$ 个副本 $K_{i,j}$（$0 \le j < R$）时，它会监控瞬时读取延迟 $t_{i,j}$。该延迟包括协调器与 $N_{i+j}$ 之间的网络传输时间，以及 $N_{i+j}$ 存储层中的处理时间，从而综合考虑网络与存储性能的共同影响。为初始化 $t_{i,j}$，协调器在每个 epoch 的前 $R$ 个请求中，以轮询方式从某个键范围的复制组中选择副本节点。随后，它基于动态侦测模块（§2.1），使用指数加权移动平均来更新 $t_{i,j}$；当前实现中的权重为 0.5。

HATS 为每个副本节点赋予一个统一分数，用于量化该副本节点处理额外读取请求的能力。对于每个节点 $N_i$，令 $Q_i = \sum_{j=0}^{R-1} E_{i-j,j}$ 表示根据最新预期状态，预期由 $N_i$（$0 \le i < M$）服务的读取请求总数。然后，协调器将 $K_{i,j}$ 的统一分数计算为 $L/t_{i,j} - Q_{i+j}$，其物理含义如下：$L/t_{i,j}$ 表示在长度为 $L$ 的一个 epoch 内，$N_{i+j}$（其存储 $K_{i,j}$）瞬时能够服务的读取请求数，而 $Q_{i+j}$ 表示预期要服务的读取请求数。因此，统一分数表示 $N_{i+j}$ 在当前负载状况下能够处理的额外读取请求数。统一分数越高，意味着副本节点处理额外读取请求的能力越强。为实现细粒度读取任务协调，协调器会将读取请求重新路由到所有副本节点中统一分数最高的节点。需要注意的是，这一过程相对于原生 Cassandra 所产生的开销可以忽略，因为 HATS 从 Cassandra 的动态侦测模块获得瞬时延迟，而且其操作与 Cassandra 已经为负载均衡执行的操作相似（§2.1）。

在大时间尺度上，由于本地访问不产生网络传输，协调器通常具有最高的统一分数，因此读取负载会收敛到预期状态（§4.2）。在小时间尺度上，LSM-tree 压实较充分的副本节点在存储层中通常具有较小的读取延迟，因此更可能被协调器选中。由此，HATS 使副本选择与压实任务分布（§4.4）相协调，以缓解延迟波动。

sections.title: 4.4 Compaction Task Scheduling
sections.originalMarkdown:
Compaction tasks create resource contention with read tasks (Observation 3 in §3.2), but are also essential for improving long-term read performance (Observation 4 in §3.2). HATS schedules compaction tasks to balance the performance trade-off between read and compaction tasks.

In LSM-tree-based KV stores, all critical tasks (i.e., read, write, flush, and compaction) primarily consume CPU cycles and disk I/O bandwidth [10, 33, 51]. To prevent excessive resource consumption by compaction, HATS enforces a pre-configured allowed compaction rate per node to bound the maximum data processed per second. This rate-limiting applies only to compaction operations from the second lowest to higher levels, as they dominate background loads, while lowest-level compaction operations remain unbounded as they are critical for mitigating read amplification from newly flushed SSTables.

**Compaction rate provisioning.** To mitigate interference from compaction tasks on read performance, HATS prioritizes compaction for key ranges with higher read loads. For each node $N_i$ ($0 \le i < M$), HATS computes the proportion of read requests for each key range $K_{i,j}$ ($0 \le i < R$) based on $E$, given by $E_{i,j}/Q_i$. It then sets the compaction rate proportionally to the allowed compaction rate; a higher compaction rate is assigned to the key range with a higher read load.

Cassandra manages all key ranges in a single LSM-tree in each node. To enable per-key-range compaction, HATS adopts replica decoupling from DEPART [55] by separating the replicas of different key ranges into distinct LSM-trees. Each node $N_i$ manages KV pairs for the replica $K_{i,0}$ originating from itself and $R-1$ replicas ($K_{i-j,j}$’s for $1 \le j < R$) originating from the previous $R-1$ nodes on the hash ring in $R$ LSM-trees. HATS applies rate-limiting to the compaction tasks of individual LSM-trees and prioritizes compaction for heavily read LSM-trees to reduce read amplification, while deferring compaction for less-accessed ones.
sections.translationMarkdown:
压实任务会与读取任务产生资源争用（§3.2 中的观察 3），但对改善长期读取性能也至关重要（§3.2 中的观察 4）。HATS 对压实任务进行调度，以平衡读取任务与压实任务之间的性能权衡。

在基于 LSM-tree 的 KV 存储中，所有关键任务（即读取、写入、刷写和压实）主要消耗 CPU 周期和磁盘 I/O 带宽 [10, 33, 51]。为了防止压实消耗过多资源，HATS 为每个节点强制实施预先配置的允许压实速率，以限制每秒处理的最大数据量。这种限速仅适用于从倒数第二层到更高层的压实操作，因为这些操作构成了主要的后台负载；而最低层的压实操作不受限制，因为它们对于缓解新刷写 SSTable 所带来的读放大至关重要。

**压实速率配给。** 为了缓解压实任务对读取性能的干扰，HATS 优先压实读取负载较高的键范围。对于每个节点 $N_i$（$0 \le i < M$），HATS 根据 $E$ 计算每个键范围 $K_{i,j}$（$0 \le i < R$）的读取请求占比，即 $E_{i,j}/Q_i$。然后，它按该比例分配允许压实速率；读取负载越高的键范围会分配到越高的压实速率。

Cassandra 在每个节点上使用单个 LSM-tree 管理所有键范围。为了支持按键范围压实，HATS 采用 DEPART [55] 的副本解耦方法，将不同键范围的副本分离到不同的 LSM-tree 中。每个节点 $N_i$ 使用 $R$ 个 LSM-tree，管理源自自身的副本 $K_{i,0}$ 的 KV 对，以及源自哈希环上前 $R-1$ 个节点的 $R-1$ 个副本（当 $1 \le j < R$ 时的 $K_{i-j,j}$）的 KV 对。HATS 对各个 LSM-tree 的压实任务实施限速，并优先压实读取频繁的 LSM-tree 以减少读放大，同时推迟对访问较少的 LSM-tree 的压实。

sections.title: 5 HATS Implementation
sections.originalMarkdown:
We implement HATS in Java based on Cassandra v5.0 [3] and the Cassandra client driver v3.0.0 [7], with 6 K lines of code modifications to these open-sourced codebases (totaling 1.3 M lines of code). These modifications enable key functionalities, including state monitoring and sharing via the Gossip protocol, current state adjustment, score function computation, compaction rate provisioning, and client-side read task assignment. We integrate the Raft protocol into HATS using a production-grade library [48].

HATS updates the expected state for read task assignment and compaction rate provisioning every epoch of length $L$ (§4). In Cassandra, compaction tasks are executed by default every 60 seconds. HATS sets $L = 60$ seconds to align with Cassandra’s default compaction interval and ensure effective co-scheduling of read and compaction tasks. We set the allowed compaction rate for each node to 64 MiB/s, following Cassandra’s default `compaction_throughput` setting. HATS adjusts the compaction rate (§4.4) via Cassandra’s rate-limiting APIs. Note that HATS preserves the LSM-tree’s internal structure for index management.

**Starvation avoidance.** Since HATS adaptively adjusts compaction rates based on read load (§4.4), write-heavy key ranges with consistently low read load within a storage node may experience compaction starvation (i.e., compaction tasks cannot be executed). To mitigate this issue, HATS enforces a lower bound on the compaction rate $compaction\_throughput/R$. When the compaction rate exceeds this threshold, HATS performs per-key-range compaction; otherwise, it reverts to Cassandra’s default first-come-first-served policy for task execution, ensuring that all compaction tasks make progress.
sections.translationMarkdown:
我们基于 Cassandra v5.0 [3] 和 Cassandra 客户端驱动 v3.0.0 [7]，使用 Java 实现了 HATS；对这些开源代码库（共计 130 万行代码）修改了 6 千行代码。这些修改实现了若干关键功能，包括通过 Gossip 协议进行状态监控与共享、当前状态调整、分数函数计算、压实速率配给，以及客户端侧的读取任务分配。我们使用一个生产级库 [48] 将 Raft 协议集成到 HATS 中。

HATS 在每个长度为 $L$ 的 epoch（§4）更新用于读取任务分配和压实速率配给的预期状态。在 Cassandra 中，压实任务默认每 60 秒执行一次。HATS 将 $L$ 设置为 60 秒，以与 Cassandra 的默认压实间隔保持一致，并确保有效地协同调度读取与压实任务。我们遵循 Cassandra 默认的 `compaction_throughput` 设置，将每个节点的允许压实速率设为 64 MiB/s。HATS 通过 Cassandra 的限速 API 调整压实速率（§4.4）。需要注意的是，HATS 保留了 LSM-tree 用于索引管理的内部结构。

**避免饥饿。** 由于 HATS 根据读取负载自适应地调整压实速率（§4.4），存储节点内读取负载持续较低的写密集型键范围可能出现压实饥饿（即压实任务无法执行）。为缓解该问题，HATS 对压实速率施加下限 $compaction\_throughput/R$。当压实速率超过该阈值时，HATS 执行按键范围压实；否则，它会恢复使用 Cassandra 默认的先到先服务任务执行策略，从而确保所有压实任务都能取得进展。

sections.title: 6 Evaluation
sections.originalMarkdown:
Our evaluation aims to address the following questions:

- How does each component of HATS contribute to improving throughput and reducing latency fluctuations? (§6.2)
- How does HATS perform compared to state-of-the-art distributed LSM-tree-based KV stores across various synthetic workloads? (§6.3)
- Does HATS effectively mitigate cluster-wide latency imbalance (Figure 2), temporal latency fluctuations (Figure 3), and task coupling within the storage layer (Figure 4), as validated by Exp#4, Exp#5, and Exp#6, respectively? (§6.4)
- How do different system parameters affect HATS’s performance? (§6.5)
sections.translationMarkdown:
我们的评估旨在回答以下问题：

- HATS 的每个组件如何提升吞吐量并减少延迟波动？（§6.2）
- 在各种合成工作负载下，与最先进的基于分布式 LSM-tree 的 KV 存储相比，HATS 的表现如何？（§6.3）
- Exp#4、Exp#5 和 Exp#6 是否分别验证了 HATS 能有效缓解集群范围的延迟不均衡（图 2）、随时间变化的延迟波动（图 3），以及存储层内部的任务耦合（图 4）？（§6.4）
- 不同系统参数如何影响 HATS 的性能？（§6.5）

sections.title: 6.1 Methodology
sections.originalMarkdown:
**Testbed.** We conduct experiments on a local cluster of 22 machines, using 20 machines as servers and two as clients. All machines are connected via a 10 Gbps network and run Ubuntu 22.04 LTS. We configure the servers as (i) a 10-node homogeneous cluster, where each server has a quad-core Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-3570 3.4 GHz CPU, 16 GiB DRAM, and a 128 GiB SATA solid-state drive; (ii) a 20-node heterogeneous cluster (Exp#8). Each client has two 12-core Intel(R) Xeon(R) Silver 4214 2.2 GHz CPUs, 64 GiB DRAM, and a 2 TiB hard-disk drive.

**Workloads.** We use the YCSB benchmarking tool [13] to generate synthetic and production workloads. The synthetic workloads are based on Yahoo’s production [17]: A (50% read, 50% update), B (95% read, 5% write), C (100% read), D (95% read, 5% write), E (95% scan, 5% update), and F (50% read, 50% read-modify-write). By default, all workloads follow a Zipf distribution with a Zipfian constant $\theta = 0.99$, except for Workload D, which uses the latest distribution. The production workload is modeled from Facebook’s real-world traces [14]. It incorporates key range locality, a hotness distribution of $f(x) = ae^{bx} + ce^{dx}$ [26], value sizes/scan lengths following a generalized Pareto distribution [28], and query-per-second (QPS) dynamics modeled by a sine wave function to capture highly dynamic patterns. We use all synthetic and production workloads for macrobenchmarks (§6.3) and use YCSB Workloads A, B, and C, as in C3 [49], for other experiments to evaluate HATS’s specific aspects.

**Default settings.** We run experiments on the 10-node homogeneous cluster. Before running a workload, we pre-load 100 M records (24-byte key and 1,000-byte value) into storage, flush all MemTables to disk, and wait until all compaction tasks are completed. We launch 50 client threads on each client machine (i.e., 100 in total). We set the replication factor to three ($R = 3$), with a read consistency level of one and a write consistency level of three for strong consistency. We evenly partition key ranges across all nodes using consistent hashing. We set three seed nodes [2] and follow Cassandra’s default parameters. Each run comprises 50 M operations, except for YCSB Workload E, which uses 5 M operations. We present average results over five runs, with error bars representing 95% confidence intervals based on the Student’s t-distribution. We use five runs since the performance variations across runs are minimal in our experiments, although we acknowledge that a larger number of runs would be desirable in real-world dynamic environments.

**Baselines.** We evaluate HATS against three baselines.

- **mLSM (multi-LSM) [55].** Instead of directly comparing HATS with vanilla Cassandra, we implement replica decoupling [55] by managing $R$ replicas from different nodes in $R$ separate smaller LSM-trees in each node (as described in §4.4), so as to reduce read and write amplifications compared to vanilla Cassandra [55].
- **C3 [49].** C3 implements adaptive replica selection in vanilla Cassandra based on replica ranking from server responses and client-side distributed rate control. We also apply replica decoupling to C3 for fair comparisons.
- **DEPART [55].** DEPART extends replica decoupling in mLSM with more fine-grained replica management. For each node $N_i$, the KV pairs for $K_{i,0}$ originating from itself are still stored in an LSM-tree, while those for $R-1$ replicas $K_{i-j,j}$’s ($1 \leq j < R$) originating from other nodes are stored in a two-layer log. This mitigates the resource competition of multiple LSM-trees in mLSM. DEPART improves write performance, but may incur read overhead when accessing replicas from the two-layer log.

All baselines are originally implemented in earlier Cassandra versions. We re-implement them based on their open-source prototypes [49,55] on Cassandra v5.0 to remove performance discrepancies across Cassandra versions [11].
sections.translationMarkdown:
**测试平台。** 我们在一个由 22 台机器组成的本地集群上进行实验，其中 20 台作为服务器，2 台作为客户端。所有机器均通过 10 Gbps 网络连接，并运行 Ubuntu 22.04 LTS。我们将服务器配置为：（i）一个 10 节点同构集群，其中每台服务器配备四核 Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-3570 3.4 GHz CPU、16 GiB DRAM 和 128 GiB SATA 固态硬盘；（ii）一个 20 节点异构集群（Exp#8）。每台客户端配备两颗 12 核 Intel(R) Xeon(R) Silver 4214 2.2 GHz CPU、64 GiB DRAM 和一块 2 TiB 硬盘。

**工作负载。** 我们使用 YCSB 基准测试工具 [13] 生成合成工作负载和生产工作负载。合成工作负载基于 Yahoo 的生产环境 [17]：A（50% 读、50% 更新）、B（95% 读、5% 写）、C（100% 读）、D（95% 读、5% 写）、E（95% 扫描、5% 更新）以及 F（50% 读、50% 读取-修改-写入）。默认情况下，除采用 latest 分布的工作负载 D 外，所有工作负载均服从 Zipf 分布，Zipf 常数为 $\theta = 0.99$。生产工作负载根据 Facebook 的真实跟踪数据 [14] 建模。它包含键范围局部性、$f(x) = ae^{bx} + ce^{dx}$ 的热度分布 [26]、服从广义帕累托分布的值大小/扫描长度 [28]，以及使用正弦波函数建模的每秒查询数（QPS）动态变化，以刻画高度动态的模式。我们将所有合成和生产工作负载用于宏基准测试（§6.3）；对于其他实验，则像 C3 [49] 一样使用 YCSB 工作负载 A、B 和 C，以评估 HATS 的特定方面。

**默认设置。** 我们在 10 节点同构集群上运行实验。运行工作负载之前，我们向存储中预加载 1 亿条记录（24 字节键和 1,000 字节值），将所有 MemTable 刷写至磁盘，并等待所有压实任务完成。我们在每台客户端机器上启动 50 个客户端线程（共 100 个）。我们将复制因子设为 3（$R = 3$），读取一致性级别设为 1，写入一致性级别设为 3，以提供强一致性。我们使用一致性哈希将键范围均匀划分到所有节点。我们设置三个种子节点 [2]，并采用 Cassandra 的默认参数。除 YCSB 工作负载 E 使用 500 万次操作外，每轮实验包含 5,000 万次操作。我们给出五轮实验的平均结果，误差条表示基于 Student t 分布计算的 95% 置信区间。之所以采用五轮实验，是因为在我们的实验中不同轮次之间的性能变化很小；不过我们也承认，在真实的动态环境中，更大的实验轮数会更理想。

**基线。** 我们将 HATS 与三个基线进行比较。

- **mLSM（multi-LSM）[55]。** 我们没有直接将 HATS 与原版 Cassandra 比较，而是实现了副本解耦 [55]：在每个节点中，分别用 $R$ 个较小且相互独立的 LSM-tree 管理来自不同节点的 $R$ 个副本（如 §4.4 所述），从而相较于原版 Cassandra 降低读放大和写放大 [55]。
- **C3 [49]。** C3 根据服务器响应中的副本排序和客户端分布式速率控制，在原版 Cassandra 中实现自适应副本选择。为保证比较公平，我们也对 C3 应用副本解耦。
- **DEPART [55]。** DEPART 在 mLSM 的副本解耦基础上采用更细粒度的副本管理。对于每个节点 $N_i$，源自该节点自身的 $K_{i,0}$ 中的 KV 对仍存储在一个 LSM-tree 中，而源自其他节点的 $R-1$ 个副本 $K_{i-j,j}$（$1 \leq j < R$）则存储在一个两层日志中。这缓解了 mLSM 中多个 LSM-tree 之间的资源竞争。DEPART 提升了写性能，但访问两层日志中的副本时可能产生读取开销。

所有基线最初均在较早版本的 Cassandra 上实现。我们基于它们的开源原型 [49,55]，在 Cassandra v5.0 上重新实现这些基线，以消除不同 Cassandra 版本之间的性能差异 [11]。

sections.title: 6.2 Microbenchmarks
sections.originalMarkdown:
**Exp#1 (Effectiveness of each technique).** We evaluate HATS’s techniques by comparing the throughput and P99 latencies of various configurations: (i) mLSM, (ii) CoarseSchedule (i.e., mLSM with coarse-grained read task assignment (§4.2)), (iii) FineSchedule (i.e., CoarseSchedule with fine-grained read task coordination (§4.3)), and (iv) HATS (i.e., FineSchedule with compaction task scheduling (§4.4)).

*Figure 6: Exp#1 (Effectiveness of each technique). All results are normalized to mLSM’s performance results (above each bar), including throughput (KOPS) and P99 latency (ms).*

Figure 6 shows the throughput and P99 latencies for YCSB Workloads A, B, and C. HATS’s performance improves steadily as more techniques are added. CoarseSchedule increases throughput by 3.3%, 67.2%, and 10.9% over mLSM for Workloads A, B, and C, respectively. However, the difference in P99 latencies between CoarseSchedule and mLSM is negligible, where CoarseSchedule reduces the P99 latencies by only 4.1%. This suggests that coarse-grained read task assignment can improve overall throughput, but has limited benefits for tail latency reduction. In contrast, FineSchedule significantly reduces P99 latencies by 8.3%, 24.4%, and 40.4% over CoarseSchedule for Workloads A, B, and C, respectively.

Note that CoarseSchedule employs dynamic snitching to schedule read requests at a fine granularity, but CoarseSchedule and the dynamic snitching module execute independently. This justifies that a holistic approach to read coordination at both coarse and fine granularities is essential for achieving consistently low latencies. Compared to FineSchedule, HATS further reduces compaction overhead and increases throughput by 32.4% and 5.7% for Workloads A and B, respectively. HATS also reduces P99 latencies by 49.0% and 9.8% over FineSchedule for Workloads A and B, respectively. Furthermore, the error bars become smaller as more techniques are added, implying more stable performance.
sections.translationMarkdown:
**Exp#1（各项技术的有效性）。** 我们通过比较不同配置的吞吐量和 P99 延迟来评估 HATS 的各项技术：（i）mLSM；（ii）CoarseSchedule（即加入粗粒度读取任务分配的 mLSM（§4.2））；（iii）FineSchedule（即加入细粒度读取任务协调的 CoarseSchedule（§4.3））；（iv）HATS（即加入压实任务调度的 FineSchedule（§4.4））。

*图 6：Exp#1（各项技术的有效性）。所有结果均相对于 mLSM 的性能结果进行归一化，包括吞吐量（KOPS）和 P99 延迟（ms）；实际数值标在每个柱形上方。*

图 6 展示了 YCSB 工作负载 A、B 和 C 的吞吐量与 P99 延迟。随着加入更多技术，HATS 的性能稳步提高。对于工作负载 A、B 和 C，CoarseSchedule 相较于 mLSM 分别将吞吐量提高了 3.3%、67.2% 和 10.9%。然而，CoarseSchedule 与 mLSM 之间的 P99 延迟差异可以忽略，CoarseSchedule 仅将 P99 延迟降低了 4.1%。这表明，粗粒度读取任务分配可以提升总体吞吐量，但对降低尾延迟的帮助有限。相比之下，对于工作负载 A、B 和 C，FineSchedule 相较于 CoarseSchedule 分别显著降低了 8.3%、24.4% 和 40.4% 的 P99 延迟。

需要注意的是，CoarseSchedule 使用动态 snitch 以细粒度调度读取请求，但 CoarseSchedule 与动态 snitch 模块彼此独立执行。这说明，要持续实现低延迟，必须采用同时覆盖粗粒度和细粒度读取协调的整体方法。与 FineSchedule 相比，对于工作负载 A 和 B，HATS 进一步降低了压实开销，并分别将吞吐量提高 32.4% 和 5.7%。对于工作负载 A 和 B，HATS 相较于 FineSchedule 还分别将 P99 延迟降低 49.0% 和 9.8%。此外，随着加入更多技术，误差条逐渐缩小，意味着性能更加稳定。

sections.title: 6.3 Macrobenchmarks
sections.originalMarkdown:
**Exp#2 (YCSB synthetic workload performance).** We evaluate throughput and latency percentiles (P50, P99, and P999) across all six YCSB synthetic workloads.

*Figure 7: Exp#2 (YCSB synthetic workload performance). We present normalized throughput and latencies with respect to mLSM. The numbers displayed above each bar show the actual throughput (KOPS) in figure (a) and latencies (ms) in figures (b)-(d).*

Figure 7(a) shows throughput results. HATS achieves the highest throughput under Workloads A, B, C, D, and F, with throughput gains of up to 1.53×, 2.47×, 2.67×, 2.90×, and 2.04×, respectively. For scan-intensive Workload E, HATS and mLSM have similar throughput, while being slightly lower than DEPART by 5.4% and 6.0%, respectively. This discrepancy arises since the small proportion of updates (5% of 5 M operations) in Workload E triggers LSM-tree compaction in HATS and mLSM. In contrast, DEPART’s two-layer log has an append-only design that avoids split and sort operations in LSM-tree compaction.

Figures 7(b)-7(d) show P50, P99, and P999 latencies. HATS achieves the lowest latencies in most cases. For Workload A, it has the lowest P99 and P999 latencies, with reductions of up to 56.8% and 16.4%, respectively, although its P50 latency is 14.6% higher than mLSM due to extra scheduling overhead. Nevertheless, HATS delivers 48.6% higher throughput than mLSM by mitigating tail latencies. For Workloads B, C, D, and F, HATS achieves the lowest latencies at all percentiles, with reductions of up to 53.6%, 62.2%, and 88.7% for P50, P99, and P999 latencies, respectively. For Workload E, HATS has latencies close to mLSM, consistent with throughput results.

We provide a more detailed analysis of the performance differences of mLSM, C3, DEPART, and HATS. HATS balances performance gains and mitigates task scheduling overhead, thereby achieving higher throughput and lower tail latencies. C3 achieves a similar balance in read-dominant workloads (i.e., Workloads B, C, D, and F), and increases throughput by 32.4%, 10.9%, 18.2%, 9.6%, and 12.9% compared to mLSM, respectively. However, it underperforms in write-heavy and scan-heavy workloads, with 2.8% and 44.4% lower throughput than mLSM in Workloads A and E, respectively. The reason is that C3 requires significant CPU resources for its replica selection strategy and incurs extra network bandwidth for redirecting requests to remote replicas. This aggravates resource contention (Exp#7). Also, Workload A generates extensive compaction tasks and consumes more CPU and disk I/O resources, while scans in Workload E require higher network bandwidth.

DEPART outperforms mLSM in Workloads A and F since its two-layer log reduces compaction overhead [55], but shows inconsistent performance in workloads with fewer compaction tasks, where DEPART outperforms mLSM in Workload C, but not in Workloads B and D. In the storage layer, mLSM reads KV pairs more efficiently as the LSM-tree maintains a more sorted structure than DEPART’s two-layer log. In the distribution layer, DEPART improves load balancing by routing most reads to the primary replica [55], while the dynamic snitch module in mLSM is more sensitive to random read I/Os and suffers from load oscillations. Thus, the distribution layer in mLSM suffers from severe load imbalance in Workload C with significantly high P999 latencies and severe load imbalance (Exp#4), while DEPART achieves 26.6% higher throughput. In Workloads B and D, the sequential write I/Os in mLSM offset the impact of random read I/Os and stabilize load balancing. In contrast, DEPART suffers from higher latencies due to network delays during replica selection and increased read latencies in the two-layer log (Exp#6). Thus, DEPART has 2.7% and 17.5% lower throughput than mLSM in Workloads B and D, respectively.

**Exp#3 (Facebook’s production workload performance).** We evaluate HATS under highly dynamic workloads from Facebook’s production [14], with 85% Get, 14% Put, and 1% Seek, and dynamic access patterns [26]. HATS achieves the highest overall throughput at 48.8 KOPS and significantly outperforms mLSM (17.1 KOPS), C3 (20.2 KOPS), and DEPART (21.5 KOPS) by 2.85×, 2.42×, and 2.27×, respectively (the figure is omitted for brevity).

*Figure 8: Exp#3 (Facebook’s production workload performance).*

Figure 8 presents average and P99 latencies per operation type. HATS delivers the lowest average latencies across all operation types, with reductions of up to 68.0% (Get), 40.7% (Put), and 41.9% (Seek) compared to the baselines. For P99 latencies, HATS shows substantial improvements for Get, with reductions of up to 83.2% (vs. mLSM), 78.9% (vs. C3), and 68.3% (vs. DEPART). While HATS exhibits higher P99 latencies than C3 for Put (by 22.9%) and Seek (by 11.0%), it achieves lower P999 latencies, with 31.1% and 71.8% reductions, respectively. This is because C3’s CPU and network resource bottleneck (Exp#7) results in lower throughput and hence less disk I/O pressure than HATS, yet HATS still exhibits lower latencies at high percentiles.
sections.translationMarkdown:
**Exp#2（YCSB 合成工作负载性能）。** 我们在全部六种 YCSB 合成工作负载下评估吞吐量和各延迟百分位数（P50、P99 和 P999）。

*图 7：Exp#2（YCSB 合成工作负载性能）。我们给出相对于 mLSM 归一化后的吞吐量和延迟。每个柱形上方的数字表示图 (a) 中的实际吞吐量（KOPS），以及图 (b)–(d) 中的实际延迟（ms）。*

图 7(a) 展示了吞吐量结果。在工作负载 A、B、C、D 和 F 下，HATS 均获得最高吞吐量，提升幅度分别最高达到 1.53×、2.47×、2.67×、2.90× 和 2.04×。对于扫描密集型工作负载 E，HATS 与 mLSM 的吞吐量相近，但分别比 DEPART 略低 5.4% 和 6.0%。产生这一差异的原因是，工作负载 E 中占比较小的更新（500 万次操作中的 5%）会在 HATS 和 mLSM 中触发 LSM-tree 压实。相比之下，DEPART 的两层日志采用仅追加设计，避免了 LSM-tree 压实中的拆分和排序操作。

图 7(b)–7(d) 展示了 P50、P99 和 P999 延迟。HATS 在大多数情况下均获得最低延迟。对于工作负载 A，HATS 的 P99 和 P999 延迟最低，降幅分别最高达到 56.8% 和 16.4%；不过，由于额外的调度开销，其 P50 延迟比 mLSM 高 14.6%。尽管如此，HATS 通过缓解尾延迟，仍获得了比 mLSM 高 48.6% 的吞吐量。对于工作负载 B、C、D 和 F，HATS 在所有百分位上均获得最低延迟，P50、P99 和 P999 延迟的降幅分别最高达到 53.6%、62.2% 和 88.7%。对于工作负载 E，HATS 的延迟接近 mLSM，这与吞吐量结果一致。

下面更详细地分析 mLSM、C3、DEPART 和 HATS 之间的性能差异。HATS 在获得性能收益的同时缓解任务调度开销，因而实现了更高的吞吐量和更低的尾延迟。C3 在读取占主导的工作负载（即工作负载 B、C、D 和 F）中实现了类似的平衡，与 mLSM 相比，其吞吐量分别提高 32.4%、10.9%、18.2%、9.6% 和 12.9%。然而，在写密集型和扫描密集型工作负载中，C3 表现欠佳；在工作负载 A 和 E 下，其吞吐量分别比 mLSM 低 2.8% 和 44.4%。原因在于，C3 的副本选择策略需要大量 CPU 资源，同时将请求重定向到远程副本还会消耗额外网络带宽，从而加剧资源竞争（Exp#7）。此外，工作负载 A 会产生大量压实任务并消耗更多 CPU 和磁盘 I/O 资源，而工作负载 E 中的扫描则需要更高的网络带宽。

DEPART 的两层日志降低了压实开销 [55]，因此它在工作负载 A 和 F 中优于 mLSM；但在压实任务较少的工作负载中，其性能并不一致：DEPART 在工作负载 C 中优于 mLSM，但在工作负载 B 和 D 中并非如此。在存储层，由于 LSM-tree 比 DEPART 的两层日志维持了更有序的结构，mLSM 能够更高效地读取 KV 对。在分布层，DEPART 通过将大多数读取路由到主副本来改善负载均衡 [55]；而 mLSM 中的动态 snitch 模块对随机读 I/O 更为敏感，并会出现负载振荡。因此，在工作负载 C 中，mLSM 的分布层遭受严重的负载不均衡，表现为显著偏高的 P999 延迟以及严重的负载不均衡（Exp#4），而 DEPART 获得了高 26.6% 的吞吐量。在工作负载 B 和 D 中，mLSM 的顺序写 I/O 抵消了随机读 I/O 的影响，并使负载均衡趋于稳定。相比之下，DEPART 会由于副本选择期间的网络延迟和两层日志中增大的读取延迟（Exp#6）而遭受更高延迟。因此，在工作负载 B 和 D 下，DEPART 的吞吐量分别比 mLSM 低 2.7% 和 17.5%。

**Exp#3（Facebook 生产工作负载性能）。** 我们使用来自 Facebook 生产环境 [14] 的高度动态工作负载评估 HATS，其中包含 85% 的 Get、14% 的 Put 和 1% 的 Seek，并采用动态访问模式 [26]。HATS 获得 48.8 KOPS 的最高总体吞吐量，分别以 2.85×、2.42× 和 2.27× 显著超过 mLSM（17.1 KOPS）、C3（20.2 KOPS）和 DEPART（21.5 KOPS）（为简洁起见省略该图）。

*图 8：Exp#3（Facebook 生产工作负载性能）。*

图 8 给出了每种操作类型的平均延迟和 P99 延迟。HATS 在所有操作类型上都实现了最低的平均延迟；与各基线相比，Get、Put 和 Seek 的平均延迟降幅分别最高达到 68.0%、40.7% 和 41.9%。在 P99 延迟方面，HATS 显著改善了 Get，其降幅相较于 mLSM、C3 和 DEPART 分别最高达到 83.2%、78.9% 和 68.3%。尽管 HATS 的 Put 和 Seek 的 P99 延迟分别比 C3 高 22.9% 和 11.0%，但其 P999 延迟更低，分别降低 31.1% 和 71.8%。这是因为 C3 的 CPU 和网络资源瓶颈（Exp#7）使其吞吐量低于 HATS，因而承受的磁盘 I/O 压力也较小；即便如此，HATS 在高百分位上仍表现出更低的延迟。

sections.title: 6.4 System-level Analysis
sections.originalMarkdown:
We conduct system-level analysis in different aspects: (i) latency balance across the cluster, (ii) latency distribution at the highest-latency node, (iii) performance breakdown in critical I/O paths, (iv) resource usage of various schemes, and (v) scalability of HATS.

**Exp#4 (Latency balance degree across the cluster).** To examine load balancing across the cluster, we use the coefficient of variation (CoV) in average read latencies across all nodes, calculated as the ratio between the standard deviation and the mean. A CoV of zero represents a fully balanced distribution, and higher values imply greater variability.

*Table 1: Exp#4 (Latency balance degree across the cluster). We show the CoV in the read latencies across all nodes.*

| Workloads | mLSM | C3 | DEPART | HATS |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| A | 0.15 | 0.17 | 0.14 | 0.12 |
| B | 0.25 | 0.13 | 0.17 | 0.12 |
| C | 0.40 | 0.16 | 0.22 | 0.11 |

Table 1 presents the CoVs for Workloads A, B, and C. HATS achieves the lowest CoV across all schemes for all workloads, with reductions of up to 29.4%, 52.0%, and 72.5% for Workloads A, B, and C, respectively. Both HATS and C3 have relatively stable CoVs across all workloads. C3 achieves the second-lowest CoV for Workloads B and C, while DEPART achieves the second-lowest CoV for Workload A. This result aligns with the design goals of C3, which focuses on improving read performance, and DEPART, which prioritizes efficient compaction. Also, DEPART always has a lower CoV than mLSM, and this aligns with the findings that DEPART outperforms mLSM in the distribution layer (Exp#2). Moreover, as the proportion of read requests increases from Workload A to Workload C, the CoVs for both mLSM and DEPART increase. The reason is that the default dynamic snitching module is sensitive to random read I/Os, and both mLSM and DEPART show high latency fluctuations.

**Exp#5 (Latency distribution at the highest-latency node).** We evaluate if HATS delivers consistently low latencies at small timescales by examining the read latency distribution of the highest-latency node. As in §3.1, we continuously issue requests for 30 minutes, in which each node reports the moving average of the read latency every second. We collect 1,800 data points per node, and select the node with the highest average read latency to present results.

*Figure 9: Exp#5 (Latency distribution at the highest-latency node).*

Figure 9 shows the empirical cumulative distribution function above 90th-percentile read latencies for Workloads A, B, and C. HATS consistently achieves the shortest tails in read latencies across all schemes, while mLSM exhibits the longest tails. Comparing the results in §3.1, under Workload B, 91.6%, 82.7%, 93.4%, and 8.7% of data points in mLSM, C3, DEPART, and HATS fall outside the 0.5× to 2× range of the average read latency, respectively.

**Exp#6 (Performance breakdown).** We provide a performance breakdown of different steps to examine their performance overhead. We focus on the breakdowns of the write path for Workload A and the read path for Workload C. Each write operation includes (i) writing to the WAL, (ii) inserting into the MemTable, (iii) flushing the MemTable, and (iv) performing compaction, while each read operation includes (i) replica selection, (ii) reading from the MemTable, (iii) reading from the cache components (key cache and row cache), and (iv) reading from the disk components. We measure the execution time for each step and obtain average results for processing 1 MiB of reads or writes. Note that the sum of the times for all steps is less than the actual processing time, as requests are executed concurrently in the thread pool.

*Table 2: Exp#6 (Performance breakdown). We show the average results for processing 1 MiB reads or writes, including the corresponding 95% confidence interval.*

| Steps | mLSM | C3 | DEPART | HATS |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **(a) Write path in Workload A (ms)** | | | | |
| WAL | 22.0±2.5 | 36.7±4.0 | 22.3±2.3 | 14.0±5.8 |
| MemTable | 11.9±0.9 | 11.5±0.1 | 11.0±0.1 | 12.1±0.1 |
| Flushing | 8.8±0.2 | 8.6±0.4 | 6.4±0.1 | 8.3±0.1 |
| Compaction | 215.0±3.7 | 210.2±2.5 | 62.0±1.2 | 39.2±6.2 |
| **(b) Read path in Workload C (ms)** | | | | |
| Selection | 151.4±77.2 | 776.6±167.0 | 263.6±36.1 | 50.8±1.5 |
| MemTable | 2.6±0.3 | 2.3±0.2 | 3.0±0.4 | 1.6±0.0 |
| Caches | 2.2±0.2 | 1.9±0.1 | 1.7±0.2 | 1.6±0.1 |
| Disk | 294.2±13.7 | 121.3±15.0 | 431.7±59.7 | 73.4±1.5 |

Table 2 shows the average execution time of each step with 95% confidence intervals. For the write path, HATS has the lowest latencies for WAL and compaction steps, with reductions of up to 61.9% and 81.8%, respectively. All schemes exhibit similar latencies for the MemTable step, as they use the same MemTable size. HATS has comparable latencies for the flushing step to mLSM and C3, but they all have higher latencies than DEPART, as DEPART directly appends redundant replicas to the two-layer log and reduces pending flushes [25]. Notably, DEPART also significantly reduces the compaction execution time by 71.2% and 70.5% compared to mLSM and C3, respectively.

For the read path, replica selection and reading from the disk components are the most time-consuming steps. HATS reduces the replica selection latency by 66.5%, 93.5%, and 80.7% compared to mLSM, C3, and DEPART, respectively. This significant reduction is attributed to HATS’s holistic request coordination, where read requests are first assigned to different nodes (§4.2) and are redirected to remote replica nodes only when necessary (§4.3). HATS only redirects 0.04% of read requests to remote nodes, compared to 6.2%, 84.9%, and 19.3% in mLSM, C3, and DEPART, respectively. HATS also reduces disk-component read latencies by 75.1%, 39.5%, and 83.0% compared to mLSM, C3, and DEPART, respectively, where DEPART has the highest latency due to the two-layer log’s access overhead.

**Exp#7 (Resource usage).** We evaluate average resource usage across all nodes in terms of CPU time, memory, disk I/O, and network I/O. We obtain the CPU time, disk I/O, and network I/O from `stat`, `io`, and `net`, respectively, in the Linux process-specific directory `/proc/<pid>`, where `<pid>` corresponds to the Cassandra process running in each node. We measure their usage as the difference between the values recorded at the start and end of client requests. We collect memory usage using the Linux command `ps -o rss`, sampled every second.

*Figure 10: Exp#7 (Resource usage). We normalize the results with respect to mLSM’s actual resource usage results (numbered atop the bars), including CPU time (×10⁵ ms), memory usage (GiB), disk I/O size (GiB), and network I/O size (GiB).*

Figure 10 shows that HATS consumes the least CPU time, disk I/O, and network I/O across all workloads, with reductions of up to 47.5%, 81.7%, and 64.6%, respectively. All schemes have similar memory usage, since Cassandra manages memory allocation and garbage collection with the Java Virtual Machine. C3 consumes the most CPU cycles (for replica selection) and network I/O (for redirecting requests to remote replicas). DEPART incurs higher disk I/Os than mLSM and C3 in read-dominant workloads (i.e., Workloads B and C) but lower disk I/Os in the write-dominant workload (i.e., Workload A), due to its two-layer log’s design trade-offs.

**Exp#8 (Scalability).** We evaluate HATS’s scalability on a 20-node heterogeneous cluster by expanding the 10-node homogeneous cluster with 10 heterogeneous nodes. Among the 10 added nodes, two have quad-core i5-4670 CPUs, while the remaining eight have quad-core i5-7500 CPUs; three have 16 GiB and seven have 32 GiB; all have 256 GiB SATA SSDs. We configure 200 M records, 200 client threads, and 100 M operations per workload.

*Figure 11: Exp#8 (Scalability).*

Figure 11 shows the throughput and P99 latencies. HATS consistently achieves the highest throughput across all workloads, with gains of up to 2.08×, 1.87×, and 2.11× for Workloads A, B, and C, respectively, compared to the baselines. HATS also achieves the lowest P99 latencies for Workloads A and B, with reductions of up to 64.3% and 48.3%, respectively. For Workload C, HATS shows comparable P99 latencies to mLSM, but our analysis of latency percentiles reveals that mLSM suffers from extremely high P999 latencies (4.65× over HATS). This aligns with the findings in Exp#2 and Exp#4, where mLSM experiences high tail latencies due to load oscillations in the distribution layer. Our results show that our findings on the 10-node homogeneous cluster still hold for the larger 20-node heterogeneous cluster.
sections.translationMarkdown:
我们从不同方面开展系统级分析：（i）集群范围的延迟均衡；（ii）最高延迟节点上的延迟分布；（iii）关键 I/O 路径的性能分解；（iv）不同方案的资源使用情况；（v）HATS 的可扩展性。

**Exp#4（集群范围的延迟均衡程度）。** 为了考察集群范围的负载均衡，我们使用所有节点平均读取延迟的变异系数（CoV），其计算方式为标准差与均值之比。CoV 为零表示分布完全均衡，数值越高则意味着变化程度越大。

*表 1：Exp#4（集群范围的延迟均衡程度）。表中给出所有节点读取延迟的 CoV。*

| 工作负载 | mLSM | C3 | DEPART | HATS |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| A | 0.15 | 0.17 | 0.14 | 0.12 |
| B | 0.25 | 0.13 | 0.17 | 0.12 |
| C | 0.40 | 0.16 | 0.22 | 0.11 |
